ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for salted lassi; closest defensible match is fermented milk drink (A0FKP). Salted lassi is a diluted, spiced yogurt beverage.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for salted lassi as a specific food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Salted Lassi is approximately 250 ml (one tall glass), providing about 105 kcal, 7 g protein, 6.25 g fat, and 8 g carbohydrates, along with 450 mg sodium and 262.5 mg calcium.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Salted lassi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from the NIN plain yogurt (curd) entry, diluted approximately 1:1 with water and adjusted for added salt and cumin. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with published yogurt-dilution estimates. Sodium is elevated relative to plain yogurt due to black salt and table salt addition. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Yogurt, plain, whole milk; FDC ID 170886) was used as a cross-reference for micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.) and University of Sydney GI Database — yogurt analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted lassi. GI estimated at 25 (low) by analogy with plain whole-milk yogurt (GI ~17–36, mean ~25) as reported in Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-3 and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (25 × 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 2.0 g, confirming very low GL. The dilution with water and absence of added sugar support a low GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from black salt and table salt addition (180 mg per 100 g; ~450 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid salted lassi due to its elevated sodium load from multiple added salts; if consumed, prepare with minimal or no added salt and monitor total daily sodium intake against the recommended limit of 2000 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25), low sugar content (3.0 g per 100 g), and moderate protein from yogurt supporting glycemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salted lassi is a suitable beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without added sugar, as its low GI, moderate protein, and minimal carbohydrate content support stable postprandial blood glucose; a standard 250 ml serving contributes approximately 8 g carbohydrates and a glycemic load of only 2.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from added salts raises cardiovascular concern, partially offset by potassium (130 mg per 100 g) and low saturated fat content (1.6 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content of salted lassi is a primary concern and intake should be restricted; when prepared with minimal salt, the probiotic content, low saturated fat, and potassium from yogurt may offer modest cardiovascular benefit, but sodium reduction is the priority.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic content from yogurt supports gut-immune axis; cumin has minor anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salted lassi made from fresh yogurt may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the probiotic cultures support gut-immune health; ensure yogurt is fresh to avoid excessive acidity that may aggravate symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Lassi (Hindi: नमकीन लस्सी; Punjabi: ਨਮਕੀਨ ਲੱਸੀ; Tamil: உப்பு லஸ்சி) is a traditional savory yogurt-based beverage originating from North India, particularly associated with Punjabi culinary culture. It is prepared by vigorously whisking or blending fresh whole-milk yogurt (curd) with chilled water in approximately equal proportions until the mixture becomes light, frothy, and well-emulsified. The beverage is seasoned with black salt (kala namak), which imparts a distinctive smoky, sulfurous aroma, along with regular table salt and roasted cumin powder (bhuna jeera) for earthy warmth. The result is a cooling, tangy, mildly spiced drink with a creamy yet thin consistency. Salted lassi is a natural probiotic beverage that supports digestive health and is traditionally consumed as a digestive aid after heavy meals, as a rehydrating drink during hot weather, or as a cooling accompaniment to spicy food. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 42 kcal, 2.8 g protein, 2.5 g fat, and 3.2 g carbohydrates, with 180 mg sodium, 105 mg calcium, and 130 mg potassium. Its low glycemic index (~25) and minimal sugar content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without added sugar. However, the elevated sodium from multiple added salts makes it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. A standard serving is one tall glass of approximately 250 ml.
common_user_queries: Is salted lassi good for digestion?
What is the difference between sweet lassi and salted lassi?
How many calories in a glass of salted lassi?

meal_context: digestive aid after a heavy North Indian lunch
rehydrating beverage during hot summer months
cooling accompaniment to spicy curries and biryanis

confidence_reason: Salted lassi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from plain whole-milk yogurt entries in ICMR-NIN and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170886), adjusted for water dilution (~1:1 ratio) and elevated sodium from added salts. GI is an analogous estimate from published yogurt GI data. Confidence is medium due to the absence of a direct approved-source entry for salted lassi as a prepared beverage.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain yogurt data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Yogurt, plain, whole milk; FDC ID 170886), adjusted for approximately 1:1 dilution with water and sodium addition from black salt and table salt. No discrete approved-source entry for salted lassi as a prepared beverage was found. 2. Saturated fat (1.6 g), monounsaturated fat (0.7 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.1 g), trans fat (0.0 g), cholesterol (10.0 mg), and water (90.5 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy yogurt data adjusted for dilution; these are approximate. 3. Vitamin A is reported as 14.0 mcg RAE (converted from input value of 35.0 mcg retinol equivalents using standard RAE conversion). 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed as naturally zero for a plain yogurt-and-water beverage with only trace spice additions; no plant fiber sources are present in meaningful quantity. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: dairy-derived trace trans fats are below detection threshold for this diluted preparation; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy yogurt data. 6. The input record listed four identical duplicate portion entries (all '1 serving (250ml)', 250 g); deduplicated to a single default serving of 250 g. 7. The input dietary_tag 'low_sodium' is inconsistent with the sodium content of 180 mg per 100 g (450 mg per 250 ml serving); this tag has been removed from dietary flags. 8. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as: (25 × 3.2 / 100) × 250 g / 100 = 2.0. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are estimated values derived from USDA SR Legacy yogurt dilution analogy; not directly measured for this preparation.
